perturbation.original_text: Such amounts shall be billed on a bi-monthly basis by Equidata and are due and payable in full by Marketing Affiliate 30 days from the invoice date. The prices set forth in Exhibit A do not include regulatory fees, sales tax, excise tax or any other fees or taxes that may be charged by states or local taxing authorities nor does it include additional fees or surcharges, including specific area Affiliate charges that may be accessed by the Credit Reporting Agencies (CRA's). Said amounts charged to Equidata will be billed separately to Marketing Affiliate and are due immediately upon receipt.
perturbation.changed_text: Such amounts shall be billed on a bi-monthly basis by Equidata and are due and payable in full by Marketing Affiliate 60 days from the invoice date. The prices set forth in Exhibit A do not include regulatory fees, sales tax, excise tax or any other fees or taxes that may be charged by states or local taxing authorities nor does it include additional fees or surcharges, including specific area Affiliate charges that may be accessed by the Credit Reporting Agencies (CRA's). Said amounts charged to Equidata will be billed separately to Marketing Affiliate and are due immediately upon receipt.
perturbation.explanation: The change modifies the payment terms from '30 days from the invoice date' to '60 days from the invoice date,' creating a contradiction with the late fee clause further in the document. The late fee is based on the original payment term, so there is now a conflict on when late fees will occur.
perturbation.contradicted_text: The Marketing Affiliate agrees to pay a late charge of 1 l/2% per month on the unpaid, past-due amount as well as a returned check fee of not less than $35.00 per returned item. In addition, the Marketing Affiliate agrees to pay 25% attorney's fees plus court cost in the event that the Marketing Affiliate's account is referred to an attorney for collection.
perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies the payment terms from 30 days to 60 days, directly impacting the late fee clause. The late fee is calculated based on the initial payment term of 30 days. By extending the payment term to 60 days, a conflict arises as the late fee clause still references the original 30-day period, thus creating a direct contradiction and confusion about when late fees are applicable. This aligns with the 'Inconsistencies - In Text Contradiction' definition, where 'different sections of a contract provide conflicting deadlines, obligations, or penalties'. The changed text 'Such amounts shall be billed on a bi-monthly basis by Equidata and are due and payable in full by Marketing Affiliate 60 days from the invoice date' contradicts the 'The Marketing Affiliate agrees to pay a late charge of 1 l/2% per month on the unpaid, past-due amount' because the 'past-due amount' is now ambiguous.

perturbation.original_text: The Marketing Affiliate agrees to pay a late charge of 1 l/2% per month on the unpaid, past-due amount as well as a returned check fee of not less than $35.00 per returned item. In addition, the Marketing Affiliate agrees to pay 25% attorney's fees plus court cost in the event that the Marketing Affiliate's account is referred to an attorney for collection.
perturbation.changed_text: The Marketing Affiliate agrees to pay a late charge of 3 l/2% per month on the unpaid, past-due amount as well as a returned check fee of not less than $35.00 per returned item. In addition, the Marketing Affiliate agrees to pay 25% attorney's fees plus court cost in the event that the Marketing Affiliate's account is referred to an attorney for collection. However, no late charges apply if the invoiced is disputed within 10 days of the invoice date.
perturbation.explanation: The change increase the late charge from '1 l/2% per month' to '3 l/2% per month', but it also introduce a new clause that states 'no late charges apply if the invoiced is disputed within 10 days of the invoice date.' This create a contradiction because under the dispute section it states 'Marketing Affiliate shall give Equidata written demand of dispute within 10 days of the due date of the invoice'. The due date and invoice date, are not the same. This also create an uncertainty on when late fees will occur.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Marketing Affiliate shall give Equidata written demand of dispute within 10 days of the due date of the invoice. The demand shall set forth a statement for the nature of the dispute and the amount involved. If Equidata and Marketing Affiliate can not resolve the dispute on their own within 10 days after Equidata receive said dispute, the parties shall jointly select an arbitrator.
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces a contradiction by stating that 'no late charges apply if the invoiced is disputed within 10 days of the invoice date.' However, the contradicted text states 'Marketing Affiliate shall give Equidata written demand of dispute within 10 days of the due date of the invoice.' Since the invoice date and due date are different, it creates an ambiguity regarding when late fees are applicable. This uncertainty directly conflicts with the contradicted section, creating an in-text contradiction as defined by the 'Inconsistencies - In Text Contradiction' type. The new clause alters the conditions under which late fees apply, conflicting with the dispute resolution process tied to the due date of the invoice, thus creating a legal issue.
